content:
# Numbers Chapter 23 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 176–177 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_023_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_023_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0130–0131

---

### Preparations for the First Oracle (vv. 1–6)

**1** And Balaam said to Balak, "Build me seven altars here, and prepare for me seven bulls and seven rams."

**2** And Balak did just as Balaam had said. And Balak and Balaam offered a bull and a ram on each altar.

**3** And Balaam said to Balak, "Stand here beside your burnt offering while I go. Perhaps YHWH will come to meet me, and whatever he reveals to me I will tell you." And he went off to a barren hilltop.

**4** And God met Balaam, and Balaam said to him, "I have arranged the seven altars and offered a bull and a ram on each altar."

**5** And YHWH put a word in Balaam's mouth and said, "Go back to Balak, and this is what you must say."

**6** And he returned to him, and there he was — standing beside his burnt offering, he and all the officials of Moab.

---

### Balaam's First Oracle (vv. 7–10)

**7** And he took up his prophetic utterance and said:

"From Aram, Balak has brought me —
the king of Moab, from the eastern mountains:
'Come, curse Jacob for me!
Come, denounce Israel!'

**8** How can I curse what God has not cursed?
And how can I denounce what YHWH has not denounced?

**9** For from the top of the rocks I see him,
and from the hills I gaze upon him.
Look — a people that dwells apart
and does not count itself among the nations!

**10** Who can count the dust of Jacob,
or number even a fourth of Israel?
Let me die the death of the upright,
and let my end be like theirs!"

---

### Balak's Protest and Relocation (vv. 11–17)

**11** And Balak said to Balaam, "What have you done to me? I brought you to curse my enemies, and look — you have done nothing but bless them, yes, bless them!"

**12** And he answered and said, "Is it not what YHWH puts in my mouth — must I not take care to speak exactly that?"

**13** And Balak said to him, "Please come with me to another place from which you can see them — you will see only the outer edge of them and will not see all of them — and curse them for me from there."

**14** And he took him to the field of Zophim, to the top of Pisgah. And he built seven altars and offered a bull and a ram on each altar.

**15** And Balaam said to Balak, "Stand here beside your burnt offering, while I go to meet YHWH over there."

**16** And YHWH met Balaam and put a word in his mouth and said, "Go back to Balak, and this is what you must say."

**17** And he came to him, and there he was — standing beside his burnt offering, with the officials of Moab beside him. And Balak said to him, "What did YHWH say?"

---

### Balaam's Second Oracle (vv. 18–24)

**18** And he took up his prophetic utterance and said:

"Rise, Balak, and listen!
Give me your ear, son of Zippor!

**19** God is not a human being, that he should lie,
nor a son of man, that he should change his mind.
Has he spoken and will he not do it?
Has he promised and will he not make it good?

**20** Look — I received a command to bless.
He has blessed, and I cannot reverse it.

**21** He has not looked on wrongdoing in Jacob,
and he has not seen trouble in Israel.
YHWH their God is with them,
and the shout of a king is among them.

**22** God is the one who brought them out of Egypt;
he has strength like the wild ox.

**23** For there is no sorcery that works against Jacob,
and no divination that prevails against Israel.
Now it will be said of Jacob and of Israel,
'Look what God has done!'

**24** Look — a people that rises like a lioness
and lifts itself up like a lion!
It does not lie down until it has devoured its prey
and drunk the blood of those it has slain."

---

### Balak's Second Protest and Third Relocation (vv. 25–30)

**25** And Balak said to Balaam, "If you will not curse them at all, then at least do not bless them at all!"

**26** And Balaam answered and said to Balak, "Did I not tell you: 'Everything that YHWH speaks — that is what I must do'?"

**27** And Balak said to Balaam, "Please come — let me take you to another place. Perhaps it will be right in God's eyes for you to curse them for me from there."

**28** And Balak took Balaam to the top of Peor, which overlooks the wasteland.

**29** And Balaam said to Balak, "Build me seven altars here, and prepare for me seven bulls and seven rams."

**30** And Balak did as Balaam had said, and offered a bull and a ram on each altar.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — The sevenfold sacrifice (שבעה מזבחת... שבעה פרים ושבעה אילם):** The number seven is the number of completeness and sacred fullness throughout the ancient Near East. Balaam's request for seven altars with seven bulls and seven rams is not Israelite worship — it is the protocol of a professional diviner seeking to compel a deity's attention through the maximum ritual offering. The Leningrad Codex (page 176, continuing from the end of chapter 22) opens this scene with Balaam taking the initiative in the ritual, establishing him as a skilled practitioner of his art. The LXX (Vaticanus, pa_0130) renders faithfully: *hepta bōmous... hepta moschous kai hepta krious*.

**Verse 3 — "a barren hilltop" (שפי / shephi):** The Hebrew *shephi* means a bare, stripped, or windswept height — a place devoid of vegetation. This is not merely geographical description but atmospheric: Balaam walks alone to a desolate ridge to seek divine encounter. The word carries overtones of exposure and vulnerability. Some translations render it "bare height" — I chose "barren hilltop" to convey both the physical landscape and the spiritual starkness of the scene. The LXX reads *eutheia* (straight/direct), apparently interpreting the word differently, suggesting Balaam went "straight ahead" rather than to a specific landscape feature.

**Verse 7 — "his prophetic utterance" (משל / mashal):** The Hebrew *mashal* is one of the richest words in the language. It can mean parable, proverb, prophetic oracle, taunt-song, or riddle. In the Balaam oracles, it designates a formal prophetic utterance — elevated, poetic speech delivered under divine compulsion. I render it "prophetic utterance" throughout to maintain the oracular register. The LXX uses *parabolē* (parable), which captures the formal, structured quality of the speech.

**Verse 7 — "From Aram... from the eastern mountains" (מן ארם... מן הררי קדם):** Aram refers to the region of upper Mesopotamia (modern Syria/northern Iraq), consistent with Balaam's home at Pethor on the Euphrates (22:5). The "mountains of the east" (*harrey qedem*) poetically describes the same region. Balaam begins his oracle by establishing his credentials — he is a foreign diviner, summoned from far away by a desperate king. This geographical framing heightens the irony: the foreigner will speak YHWH's words.

**Verse 8 — "How can I curse what God has not cursed?" (מה אקב לא קבה אל):** This is the theological heart of the first oracle. The Hebrew uses the verb *qavav* (to curse, execrate) — a different and stronger word than the *'arar* of Genesis 3 or the *qalal* of common speech. Balaam's rhetorical question is devastating: cursing requires divine authorization, and God has withheld it. The parallel verb *za'am* (to denounce, be indignant) intensifies the point — not only has God not cursed Israel, he has not even expressed displeasure toward them.

**Verse 9 — "a people that dwells apart" (הן עם לבדד ישכן):** This verse articulates Israel's theological distinctiveness. The word *levadad* (alone, apart) is not isolation but consecration — Israel is separated from the nations not by geography but by divine calling. The phrase *uvagoyim lo' yitkhashav* (and among the nations does not reckon itself) means Israel cannot be classified among the ordinary peoples. This is one of the most cited verses in Jewish theology regarding Israel's unique status. The LXX renders *laos monos katoikēsei* (a people that will dwell alone), preserving the force.

**Verse 10 — "Who can count the dust of Jacob?" (מי מנה עפר יעקב):** This echoes the patriarchal promise to Abraham (Gen 13:16) — "I will make your descendants like the dust of the earth." Balaam, the foreign diviner, unwittingly confirms God's covenant promises. The "fourth part" (*rova'*) of Israel is a unique expression — perhaps referring to a fourth of the camp (since Israel camped in four divisions of three tribes each), or simply a poetic way of saying "even a fraction." The LXX reads *tis exēkribsato to sperma Iakōb* (who has exactly counted the seed of Jacob?).

**Verse 10 — "Let me die the death of the upright" (תמת נפשי מות ישרים):** Balaam's closing wish is profoundly ironic. He desires the blessed end of the righteous (*yesharim*) but is unwilling to live their life. The word *yesharim* (upright, straight ones) may also contain a wordplay on *Yeshurun*, a poetic name for Israel. This is the tragedy of Balaam in miniature: he sees clearly, speaks truly, yet cannot bring himself to align his life with what he knows to be right. Tradition records his death by the sword at Israel's hand (Num 31:8).

**Verse 11 — "you have done nothing but bless them, yes, bless them" (ברך ברכת / barekh barakhta):** The Hebrew uses the infinitive absolute *barekh barakhta* — "blessing you have blessed." Per the binding convention of doubling, Balak's outrage is rendered with the repeated emphasis: "bless them, yes, bless them!" Balak's fury is palpable — he paid for a curse and received its exact opposite.

**Verse 13 — "you will see only the outer edge of them" (אפס קצהו תראה):** Balak's strategy is revealing. He believes that if Balaam sees only a portion of Israel — just the fringe of the camp — rather than the overwhelming whole, the psychological effect will be different, and the curse might succeed. The word *'ephes* (end, nothing, only) combined with *qatsehu* (its edge) suggests Balak thinks the visual impression of Israel's vastness is what prevented the curse. He misunderstands entirely: the blessing is theological, not psychological.

**Verse 14 — "the field of Zophim, to the top of Pisgah" (שדה צפים אל ראש הפסגה):** The "field of the watchers" (*sedeh tsophim*) is a lookout point — the name itself comes from *tsaphah* (to watch, to look out). Pisgah is the ridge from which Moses will later view the promised land (Deut 34:1). The location is heavy with narrative foresight. The Leningrad Codex on page 176 presents this section clearly in the three-column format.

**Verse 19 — "God is not a human being, that he should lie" (לא איש אל ויכזב):** This is one of the most theologically significant verses in the Torah. The Hebrew structure is emphatic: *lo' 'ish 'El* — "not a man, God!" The word *kazav* (to lie, to deceive) is a strong term. The parallel *ben-'adam* (son of man/son of Adam) universalizes the contrast — God is categorically different from all human beings in his faithfulness. The verb *nikham* (to change one's mind, to relent, to repent) is the same word used of God "regretting" making humanity in Genesis 6:6, yet here Balaam declares God does not do this with respect to his blessings on Israel. The LXX renders *ouch hōs anthrōpos ho theos* (God is not as a human), preserving the categorical distinction.

**Verse 20 — "He has blessed, and I cannot reverse it" (ברך וברך ולא אשיבנה):** The Hebrew reads literally "He has blessed, and he has blessed, and I cannot turn it back." The repetition of *berekh* emphasizes the settled, irreversible nature of God's blessing. The verb *'ashivennah* (I will turn it back/reverse it) uses the *hiphil* of *shuv* with an object suffix — Balaam is saying he literally cannot cause the blessing to return, to un-happen. This is the strongest possible declaration of the permanence of divine blessing.

**Verse 21 — "He has not looked on wrongdoing in Jacob" (לא הביט און ביעקב):** The Hebrew *'aven* means iniquity, wickedness, or trouble. The verb *hibbith* (to look upon, to gaze at) here means to fix one's gaze on with the intent of acting — God has not scrutinized Israel's sin in a way that would lead to judgment. This does not mean Israel is sinless but that God has chosen not to treat their sin as the determining factor. The parallel term *'amal* (trouble, misery, toil) reinforces this: God does not see Israel as defined by its failures. The LXX reads *ouk estai mochthios en Iakōb* (there will be no misery in Jacob), shifting slightly from God's perception to Israel's experience.

**Verse 21 — "the shout of a king" (תרועת מלך / teru'at melekh):** This phrase is enigmatic and powerful. *Teru'ah* is a war cry, a trumpet blast, or an acclamation — the sound of a people rallying around their sovereign. "A king" (*melekh*) here refers to YHWH as Israel's divine king. The presence of God among Israel is not quiet or hidden; it is announced with the clamor of royal acclamation. This is the earliest explicit reference to YHWH as king of Israel in the Torah, and it comes from the mouth of a pagan diviner.

**Verse 22 — "strength like the wild ox" (כתועפת ראם / keto'afot re'em):** The Hebrew *to'afot* is a rare word meaning "horns" or "strength" — literally "lofty projections." The *re'em* is the wild aurochs (now extinct), an animal of legendary power in the ancient Near East. The image is of irresistible, untameable strength. God's power on behalf of Israel is not domesticated; it is wild, primal, and terrifying. The LXX uses *doxa monokerotos* (glory of a unicorn/one-horned beast), reflecting the ancient uncertainty about the identity of the *re'em*.

**Verse 23 — "no sorcery... no divination" (לא נחש ביעקב ולא קסם בישראל):** This verse is deeply ironic coming from Balaam, a professional practitioner of *nakhash* (sorcery/omen-reading) and *qesem* (divination). The point is not that these arts do not exist but that they have no power *against* Israel. The preposition *be-* here means "against" or "in" — either no sorcery works against Jacob, or there is no need for sorcery within Jacob (because God communicates directly). Both readings are theologically rich. I follow the primary sense: no hostile magic can prevail against a people whom God has blessed.

**Verse 23 — "Look what God has done!" (מה פעל אל):** The phrase *mah pa'al 'El* shifts from negative (what cannot be done against Israel) to positive (what God has accomplished for them). The exclamatory *mah* (what!) transforms the statement into a cry of astonishment. At the right time, even the nations will be forced to acknowledge God's mighty acts on Israel's behalf.

**Verse 24 — "rises like a lioness... lifts itself up like a lion" (כלביא יקום וכארי יתנשא):** The Hebrew uses two different words for lion: *lavi'* (lioness, or lion in its prime) and *'ari* (lion — the standard term). The lioness is the hunter; the lion is the sovereign predator. Together they depict Israel as an apex predator that, once roused, cannot be stopped until it has completely consumed its prey. The gruesome detail of drinking blood (*dam khalalim* — blood of the slain) emphasizes the totality of victory. This imagery will expand dramatically in the third and fourth oracles (chapter 24).

**Verse 25 — "If you will not curse them at all, then at least do not bless them at all!" (גם קב לא תקבנו גם ברך לא תברכנו):** Balak's desperation produces a grammatically doubled plea. Both clauses use the infinitive absolute construction: *qov lo' tiqqovvennu... barekh lo' tevarkhvennu* — "cursing you must not curse them, and blessing you must not bless them!" Balak has shifted from demanding a curse to begging for mere neutrality — if Balaam cannot curse, at least let him stop blessing. The trajectory of Balak's expectations is steadily downward.

**Verse 28 — "the top of Peor, which overlooks the wasteland" (ראש הפעור הנשקף על פני הישמון):** Peor (*pe'or*) is a mountain associated with the Moabite deity Baal-Peor — the same site that will become the location of Israel's catastrophic apostasy in chapter 25. The verb *nishqaph* (to overlook, gaze down upon) and *yeshimon* (wasteland, desolation) create an ominous landscape. Balak has moved from Bamoth-Baal (22:41) to the field of Zophim on Pisgah (23:14) to the top of Peor — each site is a high place with a different vantage, as if changing the angle of vision could change the content of divine speech. The Leningrad Codex on page 177 carries this narrative forward toward the third and fourth oracles of chapter 24.

**On the poetic structure of the oracles:** The two oracles in this chapter display classic Hebrew parallelism. The first oracle (vv. 7–10) moves from geographical origin to theological declaration to eschatological wish. The second oracle (vv. 18–24) moves from divine character to divine action to martial imagery. Each oracle is more expansive and more specific than the last — a pattern that will continue through the remaining oracles in chapter 24. The poetry is elevated above the surrounding prose narrative, marked in the Leningrad Codex with distinctive spacing and lineation.

**On the Leningrad Codex manuscript (pages 176–177):** The text of Numbers 23 spans from the lower portion of page 176 (where chapter 22 concludes) into page 177, presented in the standard three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. The *masorah parva* annotations are visible in the intercolumnar margins, and the *masorah magna* runs along the upper and lower margins. The poetic sections (the oracles) are distinguished from the surrounding narrative prose by their layout. The scribal hand is consistent and clearly legible throughout.

**On the Vaticanus LXX (pa_0130–0131):** The Greek text of Numbers 23 begins on pa_0130 (continuing from the end of chapter 22) and extends into pa_0131. The chapter section marker (ΝΑ and ΝΒ for the LXX section numbers) is visible in the left margin. The Vaticanus text follows the Hebrew closely in this chapter, with the most significant difference being the rendering of YHWH as *Kyrios* throughout. The uncial script runs continuously without word breaks, in the characteristic fourth-century format. The Greek translation of the poetic oracles is notably faithful to the Hebrew parallelism, preserving the structure of the *mashal* form.

— PNEUMA
